verses.text: When all the kings on this side Jordan, in the hills, valleys, and all the coasts of the great sea over against Lebanon — the Hittite, Amorite, Canaanite, Perizzite, Hivite, and Jebusite — heard thereof, they gathered themselves together to fight with Joshua and with Israel, with one accord. Guidance: A coalition with one accord — six kingdoms that normally feud, briefly united by a common enemy. The terror that began in Rahab's window has now organized itself into an army. Yet the LORD has a different response in store: He divides the coalition by sending one tribe a wholly different idea (v3).

Guidance: A coalition with one accord — six kingdoms that normally feud, briefly united by a common enemy. The terror that began in Rahab's window has now organized itself into an army. Yet the LORD has a different response in store: He divides the coalition by sending one tribe a wholly different idea (v3).

When the inhabitants of Gibeon heard what Joshua had done unto Jericho and to Ai, they did work wilily, went and made as if they had been ambassadors, took old sacks upon their asses, and wine bottles, old, rent, bound up; and old shoes and clouted upon their feet, and old garments upon them; all the bread of their provision was dry and mouldy. Guidance: Gibeon is the smart Canaanite city — the one that reads the news instead of starting a war. They cannot match Israel in arms, so they try wits. Every prop is staged: bread baked stale, bottles deliberately split, shoes patched. Like Rahab, they grasp for survival; unlike Rahab, they do it by lying about who they are, not by trusting in the LORD. The same fear of God produces two opposite shapes of faith — one open, one disguised.

They went to Joshua at the camp at Gilgal, said unto him and to the men of Israel: We be come from a far country: now therefore make ye a league with us. The men of Israel said unto the Hivites: Peradventure ye dwell among us; how shall we make a league with you? They said unto Joshua: We are thy servants. Joshua said, Who are ye? and from whence come ye? Guidance: Even Israel smells something. 'Peradventure ye dwell among us' — they ask the right question. But the Gibeonites answer not with their address, but with a posture: 'We are thy servants.' Flattery deflects scrutiny. Joshua's two follow-up questions — Who? Whence? — are answered by their long speech but never actually answered. Watch what people refuse to specify.

They said unto him: From a very far country thy servants are come because of the name of the LORD thy God: for we have heard the fame of him, and all that he did in Egypt, and to the two kings of the Amorites beyond Jordan — Sihon king of Heshbon, and Og king of Bashan at Ashtaroth. Wherefore our elders and all the inhabitants of our country spake to us, Take victuals for the journey and go to meet them, We are your servants: now make ye a league with us. This our bread we took hot for our provision out of our houses on the day we came forth to go unto you; now, behold, it is dry and mouldy: these bottles of wine, which we filled, were new; behold, they be rent: these our garments and our shoes are become old by reason of the very long journey. Guidance: Notice what they leave out: the Jordan and Jericho. Their list of God's deeds stops at events from years before Israel even crossed the river. Real distant listeners would mention what just happened; only deceivers carefully edit a list. They name Sihon and Og — battles forty years and decades old respectively — never Jericho six weeks prior. A careful lie always contains its own fingerprint.

The men took of their victuals, and asked not counsel at the mouth of the LORD. Joshua made peace with them, and made a league with them, to let them live: the princes of the congregation sware unto them. Guidance: Eleven words: 'asked not counsel at the mouth of the LORD.' This is the chapter's hinge. They could have spread the moldy bread before the LORD and waited five minutes. They didn't. After Jericho's silent procession and the Ai recovery, after the altar at Ebal, the leaders make the one move the priestly Urim was designed for — and skip it. Prayerlessness opens the door deception walks through.

Prayerlessness opens the door deception walks through.

At the end of three days after they had made a league with them, they heard that they were their neighbours, and that they dwelt among them. The children of Israel journeyed, and came unto their cities on the third day. Now their cities were Gibeon, and Chephirah, and Beeroth, and Kirjathjearim. Guidance: Three days — the same span that took them from Jordan's bank to Jericho. The truth catches up to Israel as fast as God's miracle had. Gibeon is only about fifteen miles from Gilgal. The 'far country' is a half-day's walk away. The world Israel was warned about is already inside the household.

The children of Israel smote them not, because the princes of the congregation had sworn unto them by the LORD God of Israel. All the congregation murmured against the princes. But the princes said: We have sworn unto them by the LORD God of Israel: now we may not touch them. This we will do: we will let them live, lest wrath be upon us, because of the oath. Let them live; but let them be hewers of wood and drawers of water unto all the congregation; as the princes had promised them. Guidance: An oath sworn under false pretenses still binds, because it was sworn by the LORD. Israel will not cheat its way out of even a cheated promise. The princes lose face but keep their God's name unstained. Generations later David will pay for Saul breaking exactly this oath (2 Sam 21). The LORD has long memory for promises made in His name; honor them even when they hurt.

Joshua called for them, spake unto them: Wherefore have ye beguiled us, saying, We are very far from you, when ye dwell among us? Now therefore ye are cursed, and there shall none of you be freed from being bondmen, and hewers of wood and drawers of water for the house of my God. Guidance: Joshua names the deception, then names the consequence. The Gibeonites traded freedom for life; they wanted to survive Israel's army, and now they will spend forever in Israel's temple-courtyard. The 'curse' is not annihilation but perpetual servitude — and yet the place of service is 'the house of my God.' What feels like a punishment becomes the longest exposure to grace any Canaanite tribe ever received.

Now therefore ye are cursed, and there shall none of you be freed from being bondmen, and hewers of wood and drawers of water for the house of my God. The 'curse' is not annihilation but perpetual servitude — and yet the place of service is 'the house of my God.' What feels like a punishment becomes the longest exposure to grace any Canaanite tribe ever received.

They answered Joshua: Because it was certainly told thy servants, how that the LORD thy God commanded his servant Moses to give you all the land, and to destroy all the inhabitants from before you, therefore we were sore afraid of our lives because of you, and have done this thing. Behold, we are in thine hand: as it seemeth good and right unto thee to do unto us, do. Guidance: A breathtaking confession. They knew the Deuteronomy text and they knew it sentenced them to death — and they chose to live, even as servants of the very God who had cursed them. 'We are in thine hand' — the same surrender Rahab made. Faith here is messy, mixed with deceit, but it has the right object. God can build with surrendered hands no matter how dirty they arrive.

Behold, we are in thine hand: as it seemeth good and right unto thee to do unto us, do. Guidance: A breathtaking confession. They knew the Deuteronomy text and they knew it sentenced them to death — and they chose to live, even as servants of the very God who had cursed them. 'We are in thine hand' — the same surrender Rahab made. Faith here is messy, mixed with deceit, but it has the right object. God can build with surrendered hands no matter how dirty they arrive.

So did he unto them, and delivered them out of the hand of the children of Israel, that they slew them not. Joshua made them that day hewers of wood and drawers of water for the congregation, and for the altar of the LORD, even unto this day, in the place which he should choose. Guidance: 'Delivered them out of the hand of the children of Israel' — Joshua saves the deceivers from his own army. He honors the oath even against the will of the people. And the Gibeonites end up nearest to the altar, the place where atonement is made. Centuries later, the Gibeonite Nethinim still serve at the temple (Ezra 2:43). One generation's curse becomes the next generation's calling.
